next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

At the county dock the blackwater lost most of its glamour. In daylight it lay under the pilings like used oil, thick with tannin and minnows and floating grass, while above it everything that made night feel illicit and possible turned back into municipal equipment. Chain-link. Warning placards. Beige fuel cage with a sticker curling at one corner. The office door with DISTRICT PROPERTY stenciled in black and the handle polished bright where too many damp hands had worked the same cheap latch.

Mina stood outside with two coffees going sour in her grip and watched Tomas through the office window.

He had put on his good work shirt, which only meant the blue one without old spray drift bleaching the shoulders. The back of it was already dark with sweat. He stood in front of Supervisor Vega's desk with his county cap in both hands, not nervous exactly, but over-controlled, the way he got when an engine started making a sound only he could hear. Vega sat under a wall calendar three months out of date, her reading glasses low on her nose and a nicotine patch lifting at one corner of her upper arm. She corrected numbers with a purple mechanical pencil. Every time Tomas said something she erased a figure, rewrote it, and aligned the pencil with the edge of the paper before looking up at him again.

Mina could not hear much through the glass and the window unit's rattle, only the shape of it. Tomas lean and precise. Vega still. Another man at the back counter, younger, county polo tucked hard into his belt, pretending to inventory larvicide buckets while he listened...
open_hooks_to_carry: Mina attempts permits and chain-of-custody procedures and is repeatedly forced to call people who remember her disgrace.
Imogen recognizes the footage could save the camp and starts treating the eel as an asset.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...all equally trapped."

Tomas barked a softer laugh at that. Mina did smile then, because the sentence was awful and exact and because the alternative was to start shaking. The three of them stood around the desk, not touching for a moment, the lab lit by one bad fluorescent and the half-shut glow of the laptop screen. Outside, the spoonbills kept at their filthy ecstatic business in the dark as though revelation were just another pressure wave moving through the colony, nothing to stop breeding over.

Mina looked again at the frozen frame of blue under black. The image could not prove itself. The sound could not testify cleanly. The state would laugh, or seize it, or both. Donors would call it miracle until a budget line was attached. Imogen would see roof repairs and permit leverage. Investigators, once they came, would smell biotech liability before wonder. None of that changed what the animal had been in the water.

That was the worst and best part. The eel had not needed a usable file to become real. It had become real in three bodies at once. Now reality sat on the table in duplicate drives, bent metal, mud drying under her fingernails, and the changed way Tomas and Rhea looked at her and at each other. Rumor had been private weather. Fact was heavier. Fact asked what they were willing to do to a thing they could no longer dismiss.

Out in the dark channels a spoonbill clattered its bill like cheap applause. Mina thought of the eel turning under those pink legs, blue writing itself through disturbed tannin, and felt the sight settle into her not as triumph but as debt.
state_deltas_required: Everyone now wants proof, but proof requires permits, equipment, and institutional trust Mina no longer has
The eel hunt becomes a camp-level opportunity and liability rather than Mina's private obsession.
